summary: Anne Wojcicki discusses the founding philosophy of 23andMe, emphasizing that the traditional healthcare system cannot be changed from within due to misaligned financial incentives. She advocates for empowering consumers with their genetic data, building public trust through radical transparency and explicit choice, and maintaining a long-term vision amidst regulatory and market fluctuations.

principles.statement: The healthcare system cannot change from within.
principles.rationale: The $4 trillion system is highly fragmented and financially incentivized to treat sickness rather than maintain health.
principles.source_quote: this belief that the healthcare system can’t change from within. I believe that pretty strongly, even today, that the $4 trillion that goes into healthcare, the spectacular fragmentation just enables inefficiencies, and that people are not genuinely driven to have change.

principles.statement: Privacy is fundamentally about choice and transparency.
principles.rationale: Trust is broken when users don't understand how their data is used. Giving them clear, explicit opt-in choices and transparency solves the trust issue.
principles.source_quote: privacy really is about choice... choice and transparency have to be really obvious to people. So for instance, we don’t opt anyone in automatically, to research.

principles.statement: Leaders have a responsibility to pull up diverse talent.
principles.rationale: The tech industry has a documented diversity problem, and those in positions of responsibility must use their time to mentor and elevate others.
principles.source_quote: one of the most important things for anyone who’s in a position of responsibility, it’s your job to make sure that you are pulling up people who are interested in pushing themselves further, and bringing up a diverse world.

mental_models.name: The Consumer-Research Flywheel
mental_models.description: A self-reinforcing cycle where consumers are given personal insights, opt into a crowdsourced research community, and fuel discoveries (papers, drugs) that eventually return more value to the consumer.
mental_models.example: 23andMe's core business model, which remained unchanged from its Series A to its SPAC, relying on continuous customer engagement to drive both personal health insights and broad scientific research.

mental_models.name: Healthcare as a Sick-Care Economy
mental_models.description: Viewing the traditional healthcare system as a financial engine optimized for illness rather than wellness.
mental_models.example: Pointing out that the system makes money on diabetes and readmissions, but has no financial incentive to keep someone 'healthy at a hundred.'

heuristics: Keep the long-term vision constant, even when short-term priorities must be dynamic.
Don't fear failure; focus on continuous learning and adapting to what you cannot control.

signature_quotes.text: So our philosophy wasn’t about running fast and breaking things, and it was a real mission-driven company. It was like, 'We really want to put the consumer at the forefront of healthcare.'
signature_quotes.context: Discussing 23andMe's approach to innovation and regulation compared to typical Silicon Valley startups.

signature_quotes.text: There’s a lot of reasons why you can make money on diabetes. You can make money on readmissions. You can make money in every way, as soon as you’re sick... Tell me who makes money, if I’m healthy at a hundred, and I will go in that path.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why she believes the US healthcare system cannot be fixed from within.

signature_quotes.text: You can almost never predict what’s going to happen. And so, the most important thing you can do is about how are you going to respond?
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on managing a company through unexpected crises like the COVID-19 pandemic and layoffs.

signature_quotes.text: The more people are actually empowered with their purse and they can make choices, you’re going to get better care.
signature_quotes.context: Sharing her positive vision for the future of direct-to-consumer healthcare.

anti_patterns.description: Trying to reform the healthcare system from the inside.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The system is financially incentivized to make money off sickness (e.g., diabetes, readmissions) rather than keeping people healthy.

anti_patterns.description: The 'move fast and break things' approach in healthcare.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Healthcare deals with sensitive human lives and inevitable regulatory scrutiny; a mission-driven, consumer-first approach that anticipates bumps is required instead.

anti_patterns.description: Automatically opting users into data sharing or obscuring privacy choices.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It destroys public trust. Users must be given explicit choices and transparency about how their data is used to avoid privacy scandals.
